questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Upon mixing a hot liquid with a cold liquid, the temperature of the mixture
options: remains undefined for some time before becoming nearly constant
questions.solution:
Temperature is a macroscopic property that is defined only when a system is in thermal equilibrium.

When a hot liquid is mixed with a cold liquid, the system is initially not in thermal equilibrium, as different parts of the mixture are at different temperatures. Therefore, the temperature of the mixture as a whole remains undefined during this transient state.

After some time, heat exchange takes place between the hot and cold liquids until thermal equilibrium is established. At this point, the temperature of the mixture becomes well-defined and remains nearly constant (assuming no heat loss to the surroundings).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The water equivalent of a body is expressed in
questions.solution:
The water equivalent of a body is defined as the mass of water which requires the same amount of heat as the body for the same rise in temperature.

Since it represents the mass of water, its unit is the same as that of mass.

Therefore, the water equivalent of a body is expressed in $\text{kg}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The specific heat capacity of a body is dependent upon
questions.solution:
Specific heat capacity is defined as the amount of heat required to raise the temperature of unit mass of a substance by one degree.

It is an intrinsic property of the substance.

Therefore, it depends only on the nature of the material of the body and is independent of its mass, the heat supplied, or the rise in temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Identify the pair among the following that represents units of the same physical quantity.
questions.solution:
Kelvin is the SI unit of temperature.

Newton is the SI unit of force.

Joule is the SI unit of energy.

Calorie is a unit of heat energy.

Therefore, Joule and calorie both represent units of the same physical quantity, which is energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two bodies at unequal temperatures are mixed inside a calorimeter. Which of the following quantities remains conserved?
options: The sum of the temperatures of the two bodies
The internal energy of each individual body
The total internal energy of the two bodies
The total heat of the two bodies
questions.solution:
A calorimeter acts as an isolated system where no heat exchange takes place with the surroundings.

According to the first law of thermodynamics, $\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W$.

Since the system is isolated, the net heat exchange with the surroundings is zero ($\Delta Q = 0$) and no external work is done ($\Delta W = 0$).

Therefore, the change in the total internal energy of the system is zero ($\Delta U = 0$).

This implies that the total internal energy of the two bodies remains conserved during the mixing process, even though heat flows from the hotter body to the colder body.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Identify which of the following pairs of physical quantities can be expressed using the same unit.
questions.solution:
Heat is a form of energy, and its SI unit is Joule (J).

Work is defined as the product of force and displacement, which also represents energy transfer, and its SI unit is Joule (J).

Temperature is measured in Kelvin (K) or Celsius ($^{\circ}$C).

Mole is the unit for the amount of substance.

Specific heat capacity has the unit J/(kg K).

Therefore, only heat and work can be expressed using the same unit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following is true for the mechanical equivalent of heat?
options: It possesses the same dimensions as work.
It possesses the same dimensions as heat.
It possesses the same dimensions as energy.
questions.solution:
According to the relation between work and heat, $W = JH$, where $W$ is work, $H$ is heat, and $J$ is the mechanical equivalent of heat.

Rearranging the formula, $J = \dfrac{W}{H}$.

Since both work and heat are forms of energy, their dimensional formulas are the same, which is $[M L^2 T^{-2}]$.

Substituting the dimensions, $[J] = \dfrac{[M L^2 T^{-2}]}{[M L^2 T^{-2}]} = [M^0 L^0 T^0]$.

Therefore, the mechanical equivalent of heat is a dimensionless quantity.
